coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dot/polyaniline composites as voltage based dielectric material for capacitor applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Coğalmış, Fatma Tuba
Demirelli, Kadir
Barım, Esra
Ük, Nida
Ünlü, Caner
Şenkal, Bahire Filiz
Dere, Ayşegül
Tuncer, Hülya
Yakuphanoğlu, Fahrettin
coredata.dc:description: Polyaniline-carbon dot (CD/PANI) composites were prepared via in-situ oxidative polymerization of aniline in the presence of carbon dots, with specific concentrations carefully controlled during synthesis. The composite was characterized using spectroscopic (FT-IR), morphological (TEM, SEM) and electrical methods. Impedance analysis was employed to investigate the transport behavior of charge carriers in the frequency range of 500 Hz to 10 MHz at 25 °C. This study provides a comprehensive analysis of the dielectric properties of the CD/PANI composite, including dielectric constant, dielectric loss, loss factor, AC conductivity, admittance, impedance, and Cole–Cole plots. The dielectric constant of the CD/PANI composite was 6.97 for 1 kHz at 0.5 V and 12.87 for 1 kHz at 5 V. The values indicate that the increase in the dielectric constant of nanocomposite with increasing voltage. This suggests that CD/PANI composite is a dielectric material whose dielectric constants are controlled by applied voltage. Notably, the dielectric constant increased with rising applied voltage and decreased with increasing frequency. Current-voltage (I-V) measurements revealed that the composite's dielectric parameters could be tuned by varying the applied DC voltage, suggesting its potential as a voltage-dependent dielectric (VDD) material for capacitor applications. Furthermore, the enhanced electrical conductivity indicates that the CD/PANI composite is a promising candidate for next-generation dielectric devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyaniline
Composite
Dielectric properties
Cole-Cole plot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM characterization of CDs. The inset in TEM image represents the size distribution of CDs obtained from TEM. The scale bar for TEM is 200 nm.
(a) FT-IR spectra of PANI, CD/PANI composite and CD (b) XRD patern of CD/PANI.
The SEM images of a) PANI and b) CD/PANI composite.
UV–Vis spectra of PANI and CD/PANI composite.
Frequency dependence of (a) ε', (b) ε'', and c) tanδ for various applied voltage of CD/PANI composite structure at room temperature.
Plot of ac conductivity vs. frequency for CD/PANI composite.
Plot of Admittance vs. frequency for CD/PANI composite.
Dependence of B vs. G for CD/PANI composite.
Dependence of impedance on frequency (lZl–lnf) plots for CD/PANI composite.
Real part of impedance vs applied frequency of CD/PANI composite at different voltages.
Imaginary part of impedance vs applied frequency of CD/PANI composite at different voltages.
Semi-logarithmic plot of the relaxation time as a function of voltages for CD/PANI composite.
NYquist (Cole-Cole) plots of CD/PANI composite at various voltages.
Plots of phase degree vs applied frequency of CD/PANI composite at different voltages.
Graphs of a) curent vs. voltage b) curent density vs. voltage for CD/PANI composite system.
Rg and Rgb resistance values calculated from Cole-Cole plots.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dot/polyaniline composites as voltage based dielectric material for capacitor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDepartment of Physics, Faculty of Science, Firat University, Elazig, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyaniline-carbon dot (CD/PANI) composites were prepared via in-situ oxidative polymerization of aniline in the presence of carbon dots, with specific concentrations carefully controlled during synthesis. The composite was characterized using spectroscopic (FT-IR), morphological (TEM, SEM) and electrical methods. Impedance analysis was employed to investigate the transport behavior of charge carriers in the frequency range of 500 Hz to 10 MHz at 25 °C. This study provides a comprehensive analysis of the dielectric properties of the CD/PANI composite, including dielectric constant, dielectric loss, loss factor, AC conductivity, admittance, impedance, and Cole–Cole plots. The dielectric constant of the CD/PANI composite was 6.97 for 1 kHz at 0.5 V and 12.87 for 1 kHz at 5 V. The values indicate that the increase in the dielectric constant of nanocomposite with increasing voltage. This suggests that CD/PANI composite is a dielectric material whose dielectric constants are controlled by applied voltage. Notably, the dielectric constant increased with rising applied voltage and decreased with increasing frequency. Current-voltage (I-V) measurements revealed that the composite's dielectric parameters could be tuned by varying the applied DC voltage, suggesting its potential as a voltage-dependent dielectric (VDD) material for capacitor applications. Furthermore, the enhanced electrical conductivity indicates that the CD/PANI composite is a promising candidate for next-generation dielectric devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are versatile fluorescent nanomaterials with unique photophysical and structural properties such as high quantum efficiency, good water solubility, low toxicity [1,2].

CDs are zero-dimensional hemispherical or colloidal nanoparticles formed as a result of sp2/sp3 hybridization, with diameters ranging from 2 to 4 nm and containing various organic groups on the surface (carboxylic acid, hydroxide, aldehyde, etc.). Therefore, CDs are widely used in various applications in different research fields, including biotechnology, supercapacitor design, and photovoltaic cells, depending on their surface groups [3,4]. PANI is a good and promising polymer due to the presence of nitrogen heteroatom formed by quinoid and benzenoid groups that vary throughout its structure. Therefore, in the current research work, the hybrid structure formed by incorporating CQDs into polymer matrices was investigated to expand the applicability of CDs [5–7]. Hybrid structures created with CQD and polymer matrices combine the advantageous properties of both materials, providing opportunities such as improved stability, enhanced photophysical properties, tunable functionality and biocompatibility. In addition, the synergistic effects between CDs and polymers enable the development of multifunctional materials with superior properties. Continued research in this field holds great promise for designing and fabricating novel carbon dot-polymer hybrid structures with tailored properties for a wide range of applications. PANI is very important conducting polymer due to its stability, biocompatibility, adjustable electrical conductivity, high redox properties, optical properties, and simple preparation [8,9]. It is widely used in many applications such as supercapacitors [10],corrosion protection [11], light emitting diodes [12–14], catalysts, adsorbents [15,16], and sensors [17]. There are excellent literatures about applications of carbon-based QDs in energy conversion and storage applications, including solar cells, battery, thermoelectric devices, and supercapacitor [18–20]. Although its stability is a drawback, PANI is a conducting polymer with a high specific capacitance, flexibility, and affordability that make it a promising candidate for use in supercapacitors. Combining PANI with other substances, such as metals, polymers, or carbon, can improve its functionality and increase its range of uses [21]. Also, CD excel in electrical conductivity, small size, versatile and adjustable surface groups, and easy synthesis, making them highly promising for use in supercapacitors [22]. Thus, incorporating carbon dots into PANI-based systems can further enhance its electrochemical performance and stability, offering a more efficient and durable solution for advanced supercapacitor technologies. Studies on CDots and PANI already indicate that these two materials are compatible. However, the incorporation of CD/PANI structures into supercapacitor research has been undervalued, and their potential in this field remains largely unexplored. PANI and CD composites are important unique materials for remarkable conducting, morphological, sensing, and capacitance properties [23]. CD/PANI applications have been used in solar cell, biomedical fields, dye removal, sensor and capacitor [24–26]. The capacitors can be used for energy saving applications. The energy storage properties of polymer-based capacitors can be improved with a conducting material. With this connection, in order to control the dielectric properties of polyaniline for capacitor applications, we synthesized a CD/PANI nanocomposite using PANI and CD. To analyze the dielectric properties of the CD/PANI nancomposite, we used the dielectric spectroscopy method, which is known to be a powerful technique that has been successfully applied to understand molecular details depending on frequency, voltage and composite content [27]. Measurement of dielectric relaxation at different frequencies and voltages gives important information about the dynamics and molecular motion of polar and apolar groups.

The present paper is concerned with studying the dc electrical properties of CD/PANI composite prepared by oxidative polymerization of aniline as monomer, HBr as dopant, APS as initiator in the presence of CD. The main focus of this paper is to present a comprehensive review of the conductive CD/PANI composite in the development of its voltage dependent dielectric behavior. The results of this study showed that the dielectric parameters of the CD/PANI composite can be controlled by applied voltage. Current-voltage (I-V) measurements revealed that the dielectric parameters of the CD/PANI composite can be tuned by varying the applied DC voltage, focusing on investigating its potential as a voltage-dependent dielectric (VDD) material for capacitor applications.

Aniline (99%, Sigma Aldrich) was distilled under reduced pressure. Ammonium persulfate (Merck, 99%), HBr (61%, Sigma Aldrich), citric acid monohydrate (99%, Sigma Aldrich) and all analytical-grade reagents were used without additional purification in the experiments.

Spectroscopic characterization of CD/PANI composite was recorded by FTIR in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using Nicholet FT-IR Spectrometer and UV–Vis Spectrophotometer Perkin Elmer (Lamda 25). X‐ray diffraction (XRD) data of the samples werecollected from an X‐ray diffractometer (Rigaku mini flex‐600) at a voltage of 40 kV, a current 15 mA. Field emission scanning electron microscope FE-SEM (JEOL JSM-700 IF) was used to observe the morphology of CD/PANI composite. The dielectric and impedance properties of CD/PANI synthesized composite were measured at 25 °C between 500 Hz and 10 MHz using a FYTRONIX 9000 dielectric measurement system. The current-voltage characteristics were performed using FYTRONIX 9000 sourcemeter and electrical conductivity versus voltage measurements of the CD/PANI composite was recorded from electrical conductivitty measurement system.

A traditional method in literature was followed to synthesize CD. To synthesize CD, 1 g of citric acid monohydrate was placed in a 100 ml flat bottom flask, which was then inserted into a household microwave oven (Samsung MS23F300EEK model) and heated at 800 W for 12 min. Subsequently, a yellowish solid product was obtained, which was dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water and transferred into a 50 ml beaker. To eliminate large clusters from the solution, the CD solution was first filtered through 10 µm pore size qualitative filter paper, followed by a second filtration through 0.22 µm pore size special filter paper.

Firstly, 0.135 g CD was dissolved in 10 mL of water. In situ synthesis of CD decorated PANI were performed chemical-oxidative polymerization method. 1 g Aniline was added 80 mL of 1 M HBr solution. Then, different amount of CD solutions (0.5 mL) was added into aniline solutions. 2.45 g of APS in 20 mL water was dropwise at 0 °C. After that, polymerization was proceeded for 18 h at room temperature. Prepared CD/PANI composite was filtered and washed with excess of water and ethanol respectively. The green-black color solid composites were dried under vacuum for 24 h at room temperature.

The CD had a size ranging between 2 and 4 nm with a uniform size distribution, which falls within the typical size range for CDs [28,29]. The chemical structure of the CD was characterized by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs exhibited typical peaks corresponding to various chemical bonds: a max of broad peak at 3000 cm−1 for –OH bonds in the COOH group, 1715 cm−1 for C = O in the COOH group, 1404 cm−1 for COH bonding, indicative of graphene-like characteristics of carbon-based QDs, 1120 and 1190 cm−1 for C–O stretching vibrations. Fig. 1b indicates TEM image of the CDs. TEM and FTIR characterizations of the CDs revealed that they were nanomaterials with an average size of 3 nm, consisting of sp2-sp3 hybridized carbons with carboxylic acid groups on the surface. (CD/PANI) composites were prepared by using oxidative polymerization of aniline in the presence of required amount of CD (Scheme 1 ). The amine group of the aniline is protonated using acid. In this study, HBr is used as doping agent and react with aniline base, forming a phenyl ammonium ion (C6H5NH3 +) at room temperature. The dots have an unshared pair of electrons on their surface and carry negative charges.7,39 and phenyl ammonium ion can easily react with C-dots [30,31].

FT-IR spectra indicated in the Fig. 2 a and b were used to analyse the functional groups of CD/PANI. According to FT-IR spectrum indicated in Fig. 2a, the bands at 1548 cm-1 and 1470 cm−1 belong to C = N and C = C stretching mode for the quinoid and benzenoid rings. The peak at 1289 cm-1 is assigned to secondary aromatic amine C–N vibrations. The band at 945 cm-1 and 788 cm-1 is due to the aromatic CH plane bending and the out-of-plane deformation of CH in the 1,4 disubstituted benzene ring. In the CD/PANI composite of FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 2b, bands are shifted to slightly higher wavenumbers.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CD/PANI hybrid showed formation of a successful composite material. Due to its semi-crystalline nature, polyaniline is known for having an amorphous structure, which was indicated by the broad peak seen at 2θ = 25° to 30° in the XRD pattern of CD/PANI. The presence of the polymer was confirmed by the broadness of the peak, which is typically linked to the π-π stacking between the quinonoid and benzenoid rings in the polyaniline chains.

Furthermore, the broad peak showed that carbon dots have been incorporated into the polyaniline matrix. Carbon dots are amorphous nanomaterials or have weak crystalline order because of disoriented graphitic nature, which contributed to the broadness of the peak in the 2θ = 25° to 30° region. A homogeneous hybrid structure can form because the carbon dots are widely distributed and do not form large, crystalline aggregates within the polymer, as evidenced by the lack of sharp crystalline peaks.

The SEM images of PANI were indicated in the Fig. 3 a. It showed that PANI possessed globular morphology with irregular shaped flaky aggregated particles which had size in the range of 50–150 nm. Upon addition of carbon dots, the morphology of PANI displayed dramatic changes. The CD/PANI mostly possessed globular morphology with irregular shaped flaky aggregated particles, however the size of the flaky particles was much smaller compared to PANI. Also, the irregularity of the flaky particles in CD/PANI increased and tubular structures were observed. The increase in irregularity and the decrease in the size of flaky particles, as well as formation of tubular structures in CD/PANI composite were observed because of the effect of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. The carboxyl groups tend to interact with PANI which caused distortions during the aggregation of PANI and that hindered formation of bigger aggregates and caused formation different geometrical structures. The SEM image of CD/PANI composite was showed in the Fig. 3b. When the tubular structures in CD/PANI examined in more details, it was observed that the tubular structures had nano-sized pores with size of 5.5–10 nm. As a consequence, the CD/PANI had rougher surface compared to PANI.

UV–Vis spectra of PANI Ssample is given in Fig. 4 a. The obtained UV–Vis spectra of PANI was in by two major absorption bands at 325, and 629 nm which indicate band formation of PANI Emeraldine Salt (PANI-ES) due to the π-π* transition of benzenoid ring and exciton transition to the quinoid rings, respectively [9]. According to Fig. 4b, for CD/PANI composite, these two peaks shift to 330 and 626 nm, respectively.

The dielectric polarization of the material is defined as its ability to store energy. The real part of the complex permittivity represents the amount of store energy. The orientation of the dipoles of the material forms the imaginary part of the complex permittivity and is a dielectric property representing the loss of energy in the material. In the applied alternating field, permeability is a function of frequency. The complex dielectric constant is represented by the following relation 1,
(1)
ε
* =
ε
′
− j
ε
″
where ε′ is the real part, and ε′′ is the imaginary part of the dielectric constant, j2=−1, and the real part is expressed as [32]. In order to explain the dielectric behaviour of the nanocomposite, the dependence of the complex dielectric constant (ε') on frequency of the composites at various voltages have been studied (Fig. 5 a). As the voltage increases, it leads to an increase in the dielectric constant of the CD/PANI composite. This shows that the electric field dependent mechanism is a prerequisite for the dielectric mechanism of the CD/PANI composite. Therefore, the electric field applied at each voltage causes more orientation of the electric dipoles in this mechanism. Such a behaviour leads to an increase in the dielectric constant of the CD/PANI composite.

As seen in Fig. 5a, the CD/PANI composite showed a decrease in dielectric constant with increasing frequency like many polymer composites and this behaviour is attributed to the Debye type dielectric distribution. That is, the dielectric constant shows a behaviour similar to that of polar materials with increasing frequency and decreases with increasing frequency until it approaches a constant value. Also, at higher frequencies, ε’ approaches a constant value. It is suggested that this is polarization processes [33]. On the other hand, F ig. 5a shows the semi-logarithmic dependence of the real part of the dielectric constant on frequency at applied voltages of 0.1 V, 1 V, 2 V and 5 V. As voltage rises, the dielectric constant also increases. This suggests that the dielectric polarization is controlled by the voltage. The dielectric constant of the CD/PANI composite was 6.97 for 1 kHz at 0.5 V and 12.87 for 1 kHz at 5 V. It is seen that the rise in the dielectric constant of nanocomposite with increasing voltage is probably due to the increase in the concentration of oriented dipoles, more polarization, and molecular reorientation. As it is well known, the charge storage feature of capacitors is related to the dielectric constant of the capacitor, and when the dielectric constant is increased, the capacity of capacitor also increases, that is, the material stores more charge. The dielectric constant of the composite increases depending on the voltage. This shows that the capacity of the capacitor to be prepared with this material will increase with the voltage. When CD composite is used for high energy storage properties, the dielectric constant of the material will be increased by the applied voltage instead of increasing by doping. This event shows that the capacitors with higher capacity can be produced with high technology knowledge.

In the low frequency region, space charge polarization in addition to polarization is critical to increase the dielectric constant of CD/PANI composite [34]. Although the initial value of ε′ is high, as the frequency increases, it is believed that the reason for the decrease in the value may be related to the inability of the dipoles to follow the field variance at higher frequencies [35]. The dipoles are oriented when alternating electric fields are applied. The time need for this orientation is named as relaxation time constant. The high charge storage property of the CD/PANI composite prepared with polyaniline and CD, i.e. increasing dielectric constant, is the reason for the increase in capacitor capacity. In addition to the high energy storage ability of the CD/PANI composite, both the high dielectric constant and the decrease in resistance with increasing voltage indicate that it is a good material for capacitor applications. The variance of the imaginary part of the dielectric constant of composite is shown as ε″ vs. frequency for various voltages in Fig. 5b. When the dielectric loss of the CD/PANI composite up to 10 MHz at room temperature is measured, it decreased with increasing frequency at constant voltage. The ε″ of the composite is 37.8 at 1 kHz and room temperature. It is well known that dielectric loss is due to interfacial and space charge polarization occurring at the lower frequencies. The dipoles rapidly adjust to match the field, resulting in increased dielectric loss at these lower frequencies. Since the applied field cannot induce the dipole moment of the composite at higher frequencies, the polarisability decreases to a minimum. Thus, dielectric values are expected to reach a minimum. The highest dielectric loss in Fig. 5b indicated in the CD/PANI has been attributed to the rise in voltage applied to composite. And the dielectric loss of CD/PANI at 5 V was found to be 365.7. It shows that the CD/PANI is more polarized with the applied field at higher voltages and exhibits a great release of stored energy. On the other hand, the high values of ε′ and ε″ at high voltage (5 V) can be attributed to the interfacial polarization developed by the dipoles, which can align themselves in the direction of the applied electric field. At the same time, these high data of ε′ and ε″ at high voltage (5 V) also imply that the CD/PANI is a good polarized dielectric material. Studies similar to the dielectric behaviour of CD/PANI have been reported. In this regards, the high value of dielectric losses at lower frequency may be attributable to the high resistivity caused by grain boundary [36]. In addition, the polyaniline/CoFe2O4 was showing less dielectric losses than PANI that revealed the polyaniline/CoFe2O4 suitable for electronic applications such as fabrication of capacitors [37,38] The imaginary part of the dielectric constant did not show a maximum corresponding to a dielectric relaxation peak at critical frequency (fc). Fig. 5c presents the variation of the loss tangent of the quantum dot filled blend composites within the frequency range of 100 Hz to10 MHz, at room temperature. As seen in Fig. 5c, the dielectric loss factor (tanδ) decreases exponentially with increasing frequency. The decrease in electron transport in the polymer matrix may explain this decline, which occurs as the frequency increases. In addition, in the Fig. 5c, which shows the frequency dependence of tanδ at different voltages, it is seen that tanδ rises as the voltage increases. The low tanδ values of CD/PANIs at high frequencies is the main use for nonlinear optical materials in many applications [39]. The dielectric constant, dielectric loss and tanδ parameters determined from capacitance and conductivity parameters exhibited more sensitive behaviour to the change in applied voltage, especially at low frequencies. The loss tangent (tanδ) is expressed as was seen in the Eq. (2).
(2)
t a n d =
ε
″
/
ε
′
where the loss tangent of CD/PAN as the dielectric material is indicated as tanδ.

The obtained results show that the ε', ε'', tanδ depend on frequency and applied voltage. The variation of dielectric constant and dielectric loss with frequency is small at room temperature and higher frequencies. This suggets that the dipolar and interfacial surface polarization at lower frequencies are known to contribute significantly to ε' and ε'' values [40]. If there is no electric field, the charge carriers are confined in different positions or states may show different dipole orientations and this shows that the CD/PANI is more polarised with the applied field at higher voltages. The tanδ or dielectric loss is known to result from charge leakage due to interfacial polarization for storage of high dielectric energy [41]. As can be seen in Fig. 5c, the tanδ values increased with increasing voltage. At room temperature and a frequency of 1 kHz, the values of tanδ were measured to be 4.64, 13.2 and 78.03 at potential of 1 V, 2 V and 5 V, respectively. The rise in tanδ as voltage increases indicates an increase in the storage of dielectric energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AC conductivity properties of CD/PANI composite
ce:section.ce:para:
Alternating electrical conductivity (AC) of the CD/PANI was measured as fucntion of voltage and is shown in the form of σac(ω) and frequency as shown in Fig. 6 . The plot clearly shows that the electrical conductivity is comprised of two components, which are alternating current (AC) and direct current (DC) components. The DC component is dominant at lower freqeucncies, while AC component is dominant at higher frequencies. Thus, electrical conductivity of the CD/PANI composite can be expressed by the following Eqs. (3) and (4) [42]:
(3) σtotal=σDC+σAC
and
(4)
σ
t o t a l
=
σ
D C
+ A
f
s
Where σDC is the direct current electrical conductivity and σAC is the alternating current electrical conductivity, A is a constant, f is the frequency and s is an exponent determining the type of AC electrical conductivity. As seen in Fig. 6, AC component of total electrical conducivity is proportionaly with fs. AC component did not vary with applied electric field, although the AC conductivity is strongly dependent on frequency. The relation between lnσAC(ω) and ln(f) for CD/PANI at the room temperature and various voltages exhibits a linear behavior at high freqeuncies, i.e., one of the common aspects of inorganic and organic semiconductor materials is that their AC conductivity increases with increasing frequency or exhibits a frequency-dependent change. On the other hand, the DC component of electrical conductivity is varied with increasing applied voltage. The change in DC component of total electrical conductivity is due to generated charge carriers by applied electric field, and this mechanism is known as DC charge transport mechanism. In this mechanism, the charge carriers jump throughout the localized states as hooping mechanism. The hopping transport between localized sites is the main reason for the frequency dependence of conductivity in polymer composites [43]. With the presence of charge carriers produced, DC conductivity increases with the electric field. The charge carriers cause a chain reaction in the composite system, which is reorientated due to the conjugated structure of polyaniline and carbon dot particles. The AC conductance of the CD/PANI composite increased with increasing frequency and showed a decreasing trend after a maximum. However, the position of the maximum was shifted by the applied voltage. This behaviour shows the extent to which the CD/PANI composite is voltage dependent and is an indication of the reorientation of the molecules. The increase in AC conductivity can be attributed to the presence of the charge carriers, and these charge carriers cause a chain reaction of reorienting molecules due to their conjugated structure. The increase in AC conductivity depending on frequency is due to the presence of charge carriers. The charge carriers cause a chain reaction in the composite system, which is reoriented due to the conjugated structure of the polyaniline and graphene quantum dot particles. The increase in AC conductivity with increasing voltage levels in low frequancy regions is related to a decrease in impedance.

The parameter s calculated from the slope of Fig. 6 gives information about the conductivity mechanism of the CD/PANI. If s > 1, it indicates that electrical conduction is independent of frequency, while s ≤ 1 indicates that it depends on frequency or ac conductivity [44]. The slopes of the σac vs frequency graphs measured at three different voltages in this study were found to be around 0.93. These values indicate that the conduction phenomenon in the composite follows hopping conduction since s < 1. The similar studies have been reported in some of our previous studies [45,46].

The complex admittance measurements for the composite were carried out with impedance analyzer in the frequency range from 500 Hz to 10 MHz, with 0.5–5.0 V voltages. Fig. 7 shows the dependence of admittance on frequency (f) of the CD/PANI composite. As seen in Fig. 7, the admittance is increased with rise in frequency for a wide range. The increase gives an information about the speed of movement of the charge carriers as shown in Fig. 7.
(5)
Y = 1 / Z = G + j B
thus, G and B can be obtained from measurements of the in-phase and out-of-phase components of the admittance. The admittance is defined as Y, where G = 1/R is the conductance and B = ωC is the susceptance. The admittance dependence of freqeuncy is shown in Fig. 7. As seen in Fig. 7, the admittance exhibited DC and AC regions. In the DC region, the electrical conductivity increases significantly, when the voltage applied to the composite is changed from 0.5 V to 5.0 V. Whereas, in the AC region, the admittance did not change with freqeucny and the AC curves plotted at all voltage values overlap and their slopes are almost same. This feature shows that the electrical polarization of the composite. The admittance is the total electrical conductivity comprising of conductance, G and susceptance, B components. The conductance is a measure of electrical conductivity. As seen in Fig. 7, the conductance is comprised of AC and DC components. The DC component of the conductance is increased with increasing applied voltage from 0.5 to 5.0 V. The Fig. 8 show the variation of susceptance B on conductance G of the CD/PANI. As seen in Fig. 8, the spectra of conductance vs susceptance indicate two regions as DC and AC region, The susceptance remained constant as the frequency increased, while the conductance exhibited an increasing trend at higher frequencies. As seen in Fig. 8, the conductance is shifted with increasing applied voltage. This behavior implies that the DC electrical conductivity increases with voltage. The conductance reaches a saturation with increasing voltage. The susceptance of the composite is related to released stored energy. At the lower frequencies, the released stored energy is changed with applied electric field. The region I of the susceptance corresponding to controlling of released stored energy with applied eletric field. Also, Fig. 8 implies that the susceptance exhibited only AC component and not the DC component. The suceptance is controlled with electrical field. This indicates that the susceptance of the CD/PANI composite exhibits a capacitive behaviour.

One of the spectroscopic techniques commonly used to characterize the electronic properties of semiconductors is empedance analysis. Thus, the empedance measurement is important for the nature of charge carriers and dielectric relaxation in a composite or material. Absolute impedance is characterised through complex impedance [47].
(6)
Z
*
=
R s
+ j X
here, Rs is the real part of the impedance, and X is the imaginary part of the impedance together with the imaginary unit (j2 = −1). Impedance is a measure of opposition to AC electric flow or a complex-valued generalization of resistance. Impedance is a measure of opposition to AC electric flow or a complex-valued generalization of resistance. Absolute empedance value gives information about the electrical polarization and conduction behaviour of the composite [48]. Dependence of impedance on frequency (lZl–lnf) plots for CD/PANI composite was indicated in Fig. 9 . As was seen in Fig. 9, the impedance of the CD/PANI composite exhibits two regions as I and II. The region I corresponds to DC conductivity, while region II corresponds to AC conductivity. In the region I, the impedance of the CD/PANI composite decreased with increasing applied voltage. This event suggests that the DC electrical conductivity of the composite increased with applied voltage, i.e., that the DC component of impedance is controlled by applied electric field.

The real part of the impedance (Rs) plots at various voltages are shown in Fig. 10 . As seen in Fig. 10, the real part of the impedance (Rs) values decreased with the increase in frequency up to about 25 kHz and Rs is almost not varied at frequencies above 25 kHz due to the electronic polarization of the CD/PANI. At lower frequencies, the real part of the impedance (Rs) exhibited a direct current conductivity (DC) behaviour which is called as region I, whereas it exhibits an alternating current conductivity (AC) which is called as region II. The change in Rs values at the lower frequencies is due to DC electronic charge transport mechanisms, and then the Rs plot exhibits dispersive behaviour and trends to be constant. The DC resistance region exhibits a frequency-independent plateau and is defined as dissipative heating region. The presence of this region is a result of the delocalized charge carriers. In this region, the charge carriers are delocalized on atomic cites in the CD/PANI, causing a delocalized charge transport mechanism. The delocalized movement of charge carriers occurs between their atomic cites, where they relax due to their successful jumping from one cite to another atomic cite.

Fig. 11 shows the variation of the reactance value, which is the imaginary part of the impedance, versus frequency. The reactance plots of the composites show a maximum for various electric field values. The peak position corresponds a dielectric relaxation with a maximum peak. The magnitude of reactance plots indicates a decrease with increasing voltage, while the peak position shifts to higher frequencies with increasing voltage. The magnitude of the peak corresponds to the number of delocalized charge carries, and the peak position corresponds to the charge carries following the frequency of applied electric field. The decreasing peak magnitude is indicative of a decrease in number of delocalized charge carriers with applied electric field. The delocalized movements of the charge carriers are caused by the shape of the reactance plot.

As shown in Fig. 12 , the peak position, Xmax is shifted to high frequencies under various applied voltages, decreased. In this regard, the Xmax decreases rapidly from 0.5 V to 5.0 V, and max imaginary impedance values shifts from 3.8 kHz to 48 kHz. The maxium peak position indicates a presence of dielectric relaxation. The maximum frequency, fmax, of the peak position is called the relaxation frequency. As seen in Fig. 12, the peak position is shifted with increasing voltage. This suggests that the relaxation mechanism of the CD/PANI is a voltage-controlled relaxation mechanism, i.e., the charge carries causing the relaxation mechanism in the CD/PANI composite to be relaxed by applied voltage. The relaxation time of charge carries is determined by the peak position of the relaxation. The τ values for the CD/PANI composite were calculated from the maximum peak of frequency values of Fig. 12 and were plotted vs. applied voltage as shown in Fig. 12. This behaviour of the composite indicates the phenomenon of voltage-dependent electrical relaxation. The change in τ values suggests that the relaxation time decreased as a result of the dipoles following the motion of the alternating field due to the dissipated thermal energy. As seen in Fig. 12, the relaxation time depends on the applied voltages. This behaviour indicates the reasonable development of the electrical mode of conductivity in the composite, whereas the peak magnitude of dielectric relaxation is an indication an accumulation of conductive material in space charge orientation enhances conductivity [49]. The impedance of the CD/PANI composite exhibits a higher value at low voltage due to the orientational and dipolar polarization in the components of the CD/PANI composite.

In order to analyze the relaxation mechanism of the CD/PANI composite, the imaginary X of the impedance of the CD/PANI was plotted as a function of the real part Rs. The plots of X vs Rs exhibited the semicircles for various applied votlages, which are called as Cole-Cole plots. Fig. 13 indicates the Cole-Cole plots of the composite. The Cole-Cole plots of CD/PANI composite exhibit a monodisperse behaviour that obeys a non-Cole-Cole-type dispersion with a distribution of relaxation times [50]. The diameters of non- Cole-Cole plots are decreased with increasing voltage. This decrease is due to the increasing reel part and imaginary part of dielectric contant as the dielectric polarization is increased with increasing applied voltages. This change suggests that the dielectric relaxation mechanism of the sample is controlled by applied voltages. The semicircle Cole-Cole plots of the composite at different voltages exhibit a non-debye type of dielectric relaxation mechanism. The decrease in diameter of Cole-Cole plots with increasing voltage confirms the semiconducting behavior. The decrease in impedance with increasing voltage is due to the displacement of charge carriers. The radius of the semicircle decreases with increases in applied voltages indicating the increase in electrical conductivity of the material. The grain resistance, Rg and grain boundary Rgb resistance values were calculated from Cole-Cole plots and are given in Table 1 . The Rg and grain boundary Rgb values are decreased with increasing voltages. These values indicate that the electrical conductition mechanism of the compsite is controlled by grain boundaries. The charge carriers are released from rain boundaries when the applied voltage is increased.

Fig. 14 shows the phase angle-frequency of the composite at various voltages. The phase angle rises with increasing frequency and reaches a maximum peak. This confirms that a dielectric relaxation mechanism has taken place in the CD/PANI composite. The relaxation peak position is shifted to the higher frequencies with increasing voltage. The formation of the peak is due to the difference in timing of the peak of the alternating input voltage across the CD/PANI composite. The decrease in peak position of the phase angle is related to dissipated energy in the composite. The magnitude of the peak is related to the current passing through CD/PANI composite.

The electrical conductivity of the CD/PANI composite is perfomed using current (I)-voltage (V) measurements. I-V characteristics of the nanocomposite sample is performed using a sourcemeter from 0 to 1.75 V and I-V characteristics of the CD/PANI composite are shown in Fig. 15 a and b. The conductivity of the sample was determined using the following Eq. (1):
(6.a)
σ =
1
R
·
d S
where σ is the conductivity (expressed in units of Scm−1), R is the resistance of the CD/PANI (in ohms, Ω), d is thickness of composite (in cm), and S is the surface area of the CD/PANI (given in cm2). As shown in Fig. 15a, the current-voltage plot of the sample indicates a linear behaviour, in which the current rose proportionally with increasing voltage. The resistance of CD/PANI composite was determined to be R = 15,837 Ω. The electrical conductivity of the Qdot composite was found to be 5.14 × 10−6 S/cm. Polyaniline chains can intercalate to CD layers and form a conductive network. This means that it allows the conduction of electrons from both components in the CD/PANI and leads to the formation of higher conductive channels.

Fig. 15b shows current density vs. voltage for CD/PAN composite system. The plot of current density-voltage indicates that the current passing through the sample rose proportionally with increasing voltage. The current density of the CD/PANI composite is found in the range of 0.12 mA to 0.98 mA. The obtained current density is in range of mA.

The distinctive electrical and dielectric properties, particularly its voltage-dependent dielectric behavior and non-Debye relaxation, open up new possibilities for tuning the material's performance in response to external electrical fields. These findings suggest that the CD/PANI composite could be effectively utilized in applications where precise control over dielectric constants is critical, such as in dynamic capacitors, voltage-sensitive devices, and flexible electronics. Additionally, the composite's organic semiconductor behavior, combined with its enhanced conductivity at higher voltages, positions it as a promising material for energy storage systems and other semiconductor-based technologies. Future research should focus on scaling the synthesis process, exploring the stability and performance of the composite under various environmental conditions, and optimizing the interaction between carbon dots and polyaniline to further enhance the material's functional properties. Investigating the composite's long-term durability and compatibility with different device architectures will also be essential for translating these laboratory-scale findings into practical, real-world applications. The monodispersive behaviour of the dielectric properties of the CD/PANI composite was determined from a semicircular Cole-Cole plot. The dielectric relaxation mechanism of the composite was found to have a non-Debye type dielectric relaxation behaviour. In this sense, it was found that the CD/PANI composite of Cole-Cole diameters has an electrical relaxation behaviour depending on the applied voltage. The real part of the AC conductivity does not change monotonically with increasing frequency for various voltages. But DC conductivity of CD/PANI composite increases considerably with increasing voltage. It is evaluated that the CD/PANI is an organic semiconductor and it can be a good candidate for applications in dielectric and semiconductor devices. The electrical conductivity of the CD/PANI composite is perfomed using curent (I)-voltage (V) measurements. The I-V characteristics of the CD/PANI composite are measured using a sourcemeter from 0 to 1.75 V. The resistance of CD/PANI composite was determined to be R = 15,837 Ω. The electrical conductivity of the CD/PANI composite was found to be 5.14 × 10−6 S/cm. The current density of the CD/PANI composite is found in the range of 0.12 mA to 0.98 mA. It was found that the obtained current density was in the range of mA.

Fatma Tuba Coğalmış: Investigation. Kadir Demirelli: Writing – review & editing. Esra Barım: Investigation. Nida Ük: Investigation. Caner Ünlü: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Bahire Filiz Şenkal: Data curation. Ayşegül Dere: Investigation, Data curation. Hülya Tuncer: Investigation. Fahrettin Yakuphanoğlu: Writing – original draft, Investigation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cage decay, near constant loss, and crossover to cooperative ion motion in ionic conductors: insight from experimental data
Phys. Rev. B
